name: Reed Mine & Walkens Mine
short_description: Historic pegmatite mines in Harford County featuring feldspar, quartz, and mica specimens. Explore remnants of Maryland's mineral mining heritage on gently sloped terrain near Jarrettsville.
description:
Reed Mine and Walkens Mine represent important chapters in Maryland's pegmatite mining history. Located in Harford County near Jarrettsville, these historic sites preserve evidence of 19th and early 20th century mineral extraction when local pegmatite deposits were actively worked for feldspar and other industrial minerals. The mines sit on gently sloped terrain at 545 feet elevation, making them accessible to rockhounds of varying skill levels.

The geology here reflects the Piedmont province's complex metamorphic basement. These pegmatite bodies formed from late-stage magmatic crystallization, creating coarse-grained igneous rocks rich in feldspar, quartz, and muscovite mica. The mineral-rich pegmatite dikes intrude older metamorphic host rocks, and weathering over millennia has exposed clean crystal faces and specimen-quality material in mine dumps and surrounding outcrops.

Visitors can examine mine tailings, prospect in exposed pegmatite faces, and observe the mining techniques used a century ago. The gentle slope and moderate walking distances make this an excellent location for families and intermediate rockhounds seeking East Coast pegmatite minerals. Feldspar specimens here often show well-defined crystal forms, while quartz and mica occur in collectible sizes.

Because these are historic private or abandoned mines, access conditions and ownership status should be verified locally before visiting. The site offers a genuine connection to Maryland's industrial mineral past while providing opportunities to collect quality specimens from pegmatite.
history: Reed Mine and Walkens Mine developed during the 19th and early 20th centuries when feldspar was a major industrial commodity for ceramics and glass manufacturing. These pegmatite deposits formed during Paleozoic metamorphic and magmatic events in the Piedmont province, with pegmatite dikes intruding metamorphic host rocks and crystallizing coarse-grained feldspathic minerals that were subsequently mined and processed.
tips: Search the mine dump piles and weathered pegmatite outcrops for loose feldspar and quartz specimens - the fine-grained tailings often weather out specimen-quality crystals. Use a cold chisel and light hammer to carefully work pegmatite faces rather than heavy impact tools; the coarse crystals fracture along natural planes. Look for white to pink feldspar with glassy luster, clear or milky quartz crystals, and silvery mica books in the 1-4 inch range. Visit in spring or fall when vegetation is light and after rain when freshly exposed mineral faces are most visible.
terrain_notes: Gently sloped terrain at 545 feet elevation with mixed deciduous forest cover. Historic mine pits and dumps are walkable with sturdy footwear; distance from parking to active collecting areas is typically 50-200 yards depending on site access point. Soil underfoot is generally stable on slopes but can be muddy after rain. Footing is moderate difficulty with occasional loose rock near dump piles.
best_season: April - October. Spring and fall offer ideal temperatures and lower humidity for extended fieldwork. Summer heat (June-August) can be oppressive in the Mid-Atlantic; winter visits are possible but vegetation obscures specimens and muddy conditions worsen footing.
written_directions: From Jarrettsville, take Jarrettsville Pike (MD 24) north toward Pylesville. The exact access point requires local knowledge or landowner permission. Historic mine locations in this area are scattered across private and formerly mined property. Contact local rockhounding clubs or the Harford County extension office for current access information and landowner contacts, as these historic sites require permission to visit.
road_conditions: Local roads are paved; access to mine sites requires parking on or near private property and walking to exposed pegmatite and dumps.
parking_notes: Parking depends entirely on landowner permission. Historic mine sites do not have designated parking; roadside pullouts may be available but are not guaranteed.
collection_rules: Access to Reed Mine and Walkens Mine requires landowner permission. Collection is subject to private property rules. Do not trespass. Obtain written permission before visiting. No commercial collection allowed. Respect all posted no trespassing signs.
rules:
Obtain landowner permission before visiting
No collection without explicit permission
No trespassing on private property
No commercial collecting
Respect mine safety hazards and unstable structures
nearest_services: Jarrettsville (2-3 miles) has limited services; Bel Air (10 miles) offers gas, food, and water.
accessibility_notes: Gentle slope and moderate walking distances make this accessible to most hikers with basic fitness. Abandoned mine pits present trip hazards and loose rock; sturdy footwear required. No wheelchair access due to uneven terrain and natural obstacles. Not suitable for visitors with significant mobility limitations.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at Reed Mine and Walkens Mine in Maryland?
faq.answer: These historic pegmatite mines primarily yield feldspar, quartz, and muscovite mica. Feldspar specimens often show pink to white coloring with glassy luster and well-defined crystal faces. Quartz occurs as clear to milky crystals, and mica appears as silvery books ideal for collectors. Specimens typically range from 1 to 4 inches.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Reed Mine near Jarrettsville?
faq.answer: No permit is required from the state, but landowner permission is mandatory. These are private or historic abandoned sites. Contact local rockhounding clubs or the Harford County extension office to locate current property owners and obtain written permission before visiting.

faq.question: How do I access Reed Mine and Walkens Mine for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Access is by foot from local roadside parking after obtaining landowner permission. The sites are located near Jarrettsville in Harford County on gently sloped terrain. No fee is charged if you have permission, but you must respect private property boundaries and posted signs.

faq.question: What is the best time to visit Reed Mine for collecting feldspar and quartz?
faq.answer: April through October offers the most pleasant conditions. Spring and fall provide ideal temperatures for extended fieldwork. Summer can be hot and humid; winter visits are possible but vegetation obscures specimens and muddy conditions make footing treacherous.

faq.question: Are these historic Maryland mines beginner-friendly for family rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, the moderate difficulty level and gently sloped terrain make Reed Mine and Walkens Mine suitable for families with children 8 and older. However, abandoned mine hazards require supervision and caution. Always verify access permission first, and keep children away from unstable mine pits and loose rock zones.

faq.question: What tools should I bring for pegmatite specimen collecting at Walkens Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a small cold chisel, light hammer, and small chisel for carefully extracting specimens from pegmatite faces. Wear sturdy boots for uneven terrain. A 5-gallon bucket collects smaller specimens. Avoid heavy impact tools; pegmatite crystals fracture along natural planes with light tapping.

meta_title: Reed Mine & Walkens Mine Rockhounding - Maryland
meta_description: Collect feldspar, quartz, and mica at historic pegmatite mines near Jarrettsville, MD. Private site requires landowner permission. Moderate difficulty, family-friendly.
